1: uenerunt: the perfect tense stresses present circumstances; the festival “has arrived.” The present narration continues with secubat in the next verse. The genitive adjective Cerealis modifies the noun sacrum, here meaning “a festival” (OLD s.v. sacrum 4).
2: uacuo: the bed is not completely empty (the girl presumably sleeps in it) but only empty of the speaker.
3: flaua Ceres: vocative. The speaker addresses the goddess directly and, in the next couplet, asks for the origin (or aetiology; see the introductory essay for this poem) of a specific practice associated with the celebration of her rites, namely, women’s abstinence from sex during the festival. redimita: scansion shows redimita to end in a short a, agreeing with Ceres. tenues … capillos: accusative of respect (or Greek accusative) with redimita. Ceres’s hair is crowned with ears of corn. Cf. A&G 397b.
4: sacris = “festivals” (see note 1) or “sacred rites” (OLD s.v. sacrum 3). commoda nostra: the speaker refers euphemistically to sexual “conveniences.” nostra may be a poetic plural, or the speaker is taking it upon himself to speak for men as a whole.
5: The couplet functions as a captatio beneuolentiae, or “seizing of goodwill,” of the goddess. dea: vocative. The direct address from the previous couplet continues. Rearranging this verse in English word order might yield dea, gentes ubi quaeque [sunt] loquuntur te [esse] munificam. ubi quaeque = “where each (exist/are).” Manuscript P reads ubiquāque, “everywhere.” The Oxford Latin Dictionary cites this passage under the headword ubiquāque and designates ubi quaeque as a varia lectio (abbreviated “v.l.”), meaning “alternate reading.
6: ulla: modifies an implied dea inuidet: takes a dative object. With minus, the verb has the sense of a double negative: “Nor is anyone less unwilling (= more willing) to grant human benefits.” bonis: here functioning as a substantive adjective, itself modified by humanis.
7: ante: a temporal adverb invoking a Golden Age of sorts. See the note on in the Grammatical Commentary for Amores 3.8.35.
8: notum: a predicate adjective modifying nomen, which here carries the sense of “term” (OLD s.v. nomen 5) or “word.” terris: dative with notum. area: the specialized term for a “threshing floor” (OLD s.v. area 2). An editor following English-language conventions might put this word in quotation marks.
9: quercus: scansion shows the second ū is long. The verb confirms it is nominative plural. oracula prima: the speaker alludes to the ancient oracle of Dodona in northwest Greece where a sacred grove populated with oak trees was associated with the worship of Zeus (Jupiter). Herodotus (2.55) suggests that oracular messages were delivered by doves in the trees.
10: haec: the antecedent is glandem in v. 9. erat: the verb for the compound subject is in the singular to agree with the nearest subject haec. herba: presumably a young, tender form of turf. The speaker emphasizes a time “before Ceres,” when people ate only what the earth provided without cultivation. For more on the Golden Age, see the Grammatical Commentary for Amores 3.8.35.
12: comas: the “heads” of mature grain before scything, or mowing. subsecuit: note that Ceres remains the subject, as in v. 14. Taken with the previous line, the verse implies that the goddess taught by demonstration.
13: prima: resupply Ceres as the subject modified by this adjective. supponere: here, as often, taking an accusative and a dative (i.e., “to place [accusative] under [dative]”). coegit: governs a subject accusative and infinitive, similar to iubeō.
14: dente: the “tooth” of a plow used for tilling (old) soil.
15: hanc: accusative subject (referring to Ceres) in indirect statement. The position at the beginning of the verse emphasizes the speaker’s incredulity. laetari: a deponent infinitive in indirect statement. amantum: a substantive adjective from the participle of amō = “of the ones loving” (i.e., “of lovers”).
16: coli: a passive infinitive modified by bene and continuing the indirect statement from v. 15. It here carries the sense of religious observance (OLD s.v. colō 6). secubitu: the speaker reminds readers of his primary grievance.
17: est: the subject remains Ceres, as confirmed by the feminine pronoun illa in the concessive clause.
18: amoris: genitive with uiduum = “bereft of…” Compare the speaker’s description (and flattery) of Nape in Amores 1.11.9-12.
19: The speaker offers a lengthy account (through v. 42) of Ceres’ love of a youth named Iasius, or Iasion. In Odyssey 5.125-128, Kalypso briefly invokes the tale to illustrate a double standard in human and divine relationships: Male gods can sleep with mortal women, but after Zeus found out that Demeter and Iasion “mingled in love and sleep in a thrice-ploughed field,” he killed the man. The speaker in Amores 3.10 does not narrate the death of Iasius but alludes in v. 42 to the brevity of the affair. Cretes: The speaker riffs on a famous paradox pertaining to Cretans. Epimenides, a native of Crete, is credited with the statement, “All Cretans are liars.” If he is telling the truth, then the content of his statement becomes false. If he is lying, then he seems to prove the truth of his statement. A version of the paradox is presented Callimachus’s Hymn to Zeus.
